---
title: "Synthesis"

execute:
  freeze: false
  cache: false

title-block-style: none

knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    dev: "ragg_png"
---

```{=latex}
\clearpage
\providecommand{\chaptermark}[1]{}
\ifcsname c@chapter\endcsname
\setcounter{chapter}{6}
\setcounter{section}{0}
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{\protect\numberline{6}Synthesis}
\chaptermark{Synthesis}
\fi
\providecommand{\thechapter}{6}
\thispagestyle{empty}
\begin{tikzpicture}[remember picture, overlay]
  \node[inner sep=0pt] at ([yshift=-3.5cm]current page.center)
    {\includegraphics[width=\paperwidth,height=\paperheight]{images/ch6_cover.jpg}};
  \fill[black]
    (current page.north west) rectangle ([yshift=-7cm]current page.north east);
  \node[color=thesissand, align=left,
        font=\fontsize{13}{16}\selectfont\sffamily, anchor=west]
    at ([xshift=1.5cm, yshift=-1.5cm]current page.north west)
    {Chapter \thechapter};
  \node[color=white, text width=0.85\paperwidth, align=left,
        font=\fontsize{22}{28}\selectfont\bfseries\sffamily, anchor=west]
    at ([xshift=1.5cm, yshift=-4cm]current page.north west)
    {Synthesis};
\end{tikzpicture}
\null
\clearpage
```

::: {.content-visible when-format="docx"}
![](images/ch6_cover.jpg){fig-align="center" width=15cm}

*What do these findings mean for the future of kōura habitat restoration?*

{{< pagebreak >}}
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
# Synthesis {.unnumbered}
:::

## Introduction
The chapters preceding this synthesis established the ecological and practical basis for using stone piles as a kōura habitat restoration tool. Behavioural experiments (Chapter 2) confirmed that kōura respond with hightend refuge use to novel predatores than native ones. Field observations (Chapter 3) identified the habitat features that support kōura along lake shorelines, and mesocosm trials (Chapter 4) showed that structure stability and complexity matter more than material when kōura select shelter. Chapter 5 demonstrated that stone piles deployed along the shoreline of Lake Rotoiti attracted kōura at densities approaching those on natural rocky substrates. This chapter presents two pilot studies  that extend the work in different directions. The first trialled the construction logistics and kōura reintroduction in Lake Ōkaro. The second extended deployment into deeper water across a depth gradient and boat-based construction. Together, these case studies broaden the application of the restoration approach devaloped in this thesis for future deployment and monitoring.

## Stones pile field trial Lake Ōkaro
In Lake Rotoiti several stone piles were assambled but before those were deployed the construction process by using a trailer and deploying the stones piles by hand on the lakes shoreline was tested in Lake Ōkaro. Lake Ōkaro is one of the smaller Rotorura Te Arawa lakes and is also volcanic with an area of 30.19 ha, a max and mean depth of 18 m and 12.5 m. Water quality in the lake has been very poor with tropic state flipping between hypereutrophic and eutrophic and frequent algae blooms [@BRON]. To improve water quality in the lake, in 2007 a wetland has been constructed to remove nitrogens and sediments off the main inflows into the lake [@Hudson2009]. Twice a year alum dosings (aluminum sulfate) are execute as in lake phosphorous treatment [@BRON]. Kōura have not been present in Lake Ōkaro in monitorings from 1981 to 2007 [@Parkyn2007] and monitoring in 2016 and 2017 still found no kōura in the lake even as water quality had been improved [@Kusabs2017]. Water quality now should be far enough restored that kōura should be able to survive in the lake but natural recolonosation has not happened yet, therefore reintroduction might be possible. Lake Ōkaro is stocked with trout for angling and as a preadaor of kōura, extra refuge and habitat by stone piles would be good to trial in this lake. The lake has no stone or rocky substrates therefore the construction of two stone piles would provide initial refuge for the introduced kōura in the lake. 

Construction of the stone piles was executed by all parties involved in the lake with people from Te Arawa Lake Trust, Ngāti Tahu - Ngāti Whaoa, Te Mana o Ngāti Rangitihi Trust, Bay of Plenty Regional Council and The University of Waikato. Stones were sourced from Rainbow Quarry located on Rainbow Mountain three km from the lake. Stones used had a size range of 70-210 mm mean 120 mm and a total of 5 m3 was was divided over two stone piles on the north east shore of the lake. Stone piles were placed 3.4 and 6 m of the shore, horizontal with each 5 m long and 2 meters wide at 1 m depth. To follow dissolved oxygen and temperature devalopment of the stone piles and how they compare to the rest of the lake on 11 September 2024 three HOBO U26-001 Dissolved Oxygen Data Loggers (± 0.2 mg L$^{-1}$ DO accuracy, ± 0.2 °C temperature accuracy; 0–30 mg L$^{-1}$ measurement range; Onset Computer Corporation, Bourne, MA, USA), were placed in the lake for a period of six months. Two loggers were buried inside each of the stone piles and the third logger was placed outside of the stone piles as control. In October 2024 twenty kōura sourced from within the catchment were reintroduction on the stone piles to start a new population of kōura in the lake. 

In the early summer of 2024 the lake stratified rapidly with oxygen levels dropping quike and at December 10th oxygen levels reaching less than 3 mg L$^{-1}$ at water surface level for multiple days (@fig-okaro-combined). The HOBO loggers at the shoreline and in the stone piles showed that inside the DO levels dropped slightly faster than compared to the control site and that the daily average of DO was lower than outside of the stone piles (@fig-okaro-combined). This is expected as the sensors buried inside the stones were able to get less mixed water and as close to periphyton the oxygen levels will deplet more during the night. For koura it is expected that they will hide only on the outside of the stone piles and emerge at night to forange. Due to the anoxic event in the lake, trout were dying and also for koura it was expected that they might not have survived this event. 

![Daily mean water temperature (top) and dissolved oxygen (bottom) at Ōkaro stone pile, control sites, and the lake buoy < 1m. Period: 2024-09-11 to 2025-02-27.](06_synthesis/outputs/fig-okaro-combined.png){#fig-okaro-combined}

## Deep stone piles Lake Rotoiti
In the previous chapters and in Lake Ōkaro stone piles were deployed from land and placed therefore relative close to the shoreline. Stone pile construction across a depth gradient needed deployment by boat. This made it possible to deploy stone piles over several depths and in invasive macrophyte beds to test if stones were able to suppress their growt. At a greater depth the monitoring using nets is harder and therefore different monitoring ways were applied before and after construction of these deep stone piles in Lake Rotoiti.  

Monitoring was executed using night-time spotlighting, remotely operated vehicle (ROV) dives, and day-time human dives. Spotlighting was executed after sunset along the shoreline and the area around the jetty, by observing for kōura using a torch. Each kōura was counted and their size estimated. The stone piles in the macrophyte bed and deeper were after sunset monitored using a FIFISH V-EVO (4K 60 fps camera; 166° field of view; QYSEA Technology, Shenzhen, China). The ROV was used to swim transects at the location of the stone piles and kōura shown were counted. Kōura CPUE was calculated over the time spent monitoring. Spotlight and ROV monitoring was executed two times before stone pile construction, and day-time diving was executed once before construction.

The construction of four deep stone piles in Lake Rotoiti took place on the 20th of November 2025. Three of the stone piles were constructed perpendicular to the shoreline, with the first one placed underneath a jetty at around 1 m depth. The second was placed in the middle of an invasive macrophyte bed infront of the jetty around 3 m depth, and the third was placed at the end of the macrophytes at a depth from 7 to 9 m. To prevent the stones from sinking into the sandy sediment, wooden slabs were first installed wherafter the stones were placed, holding the wooden slabs in place. The fourth stone pile was constructed on a bedrock shelf 120 m away from the other stone piles. As these stones were placed on bedrock no wooden slabs were needed. Construction was executed by a boat with a winch lowering bags of stones to the designated location and empty by divers. 

The first pre monitoring (30/06/2025; water temp 13 °C) resulted in two kōura sightings by spotlighting and two by the ROV, second pre monitoring (26/08/2025; water temp 11 °C) showed zero kōura by spotlighting but seven with the ROV. The human dive did not found any kōura before construction (10/11/2025; water temp 18.7 °C). The monitoring after construction (1/05/2026; water temp 16 °C) revealed ten kōura by spotlighting around the jetty and eleven kōura on the deeper stone piles monitored by the ROV. Most kōura were seen at the border of the stone piles where they met with the sandy substrate this was expected as visual it was hard to look inside the stone piles and kōura were observed when emerged and started foraging. The stone pile in the macrophyte bed showed a complete block of macrophyte growt but at the sides the machrophyes were present densely making kōura observations difficult. The human diving did not found any kōura (15/01/2026; water temp 21.7 °C) but noticed many bullies around the stone piles. The second human dive (28/07/2026; water temp ... °C) found ... kōura. 

As kōura are nocturnal daytime monitoring is expected to receive lower kōura counts as they are assumably hidden away between the stones and its not expected that the kōura will be observed outside of their shelter during the day. Footage of the ROV showed that despite the noise of the engines the kōura do submerge from their hiding place and they do not look affected by the monitoring of the ROV. 


## Conclusion
Construction of stone piles on the shoreline by hand is a very cheap and easy way of building extra structure and refuge for kōura on the shoreline. The reintroduction of kōura in Lake Ōkaro was due to strong anoxydation event made difficult. Nevertheless the lessons learned were of great value for the further devalopment of the stone pile structure as the stone size in Ōkaro was smaller (range 70-210 mm;mean 120 mm) leading to more effort of getting the stones from the trailer into the lake and smaller stones provide smaller crevich sizes limiting shelter availability for larger size kōura. For deployment in Lake Rotoiti slightly larger stones (85 - 315 mm; mean 173 mm) were therefore selected to build the stone piles. This size range was very cconvineitn as the size of the stones was requiring machinery for lifting which gave the opportunity to trow the stones further than the end of the trailer and made it possible to rearange the stones if needed. 

Placing stone piles at various depths revealed that stone piles do quickly attract kōura from the surroundings and are able to suppress invasive macrophytes. By placing stone piles at several depths kōura can shift to hiding places most suited at specific environmental conditions.
